Tutorial 9: Validation

QGM Team

21:52:01 on 14 February, 2023

1 Introduction

In this tutorial we will look at classical cross-validation. This is where the data is randomly split up into k sets (for k-fold cross-validation) and the model is fitted to (k-1) sets as a training set and the parameters used to predict the phenotypes of the remaining validation set. This is repeated so that each set has been used as a validation set once and been included in the training set (k-1) times. Each time we assess the quality of the prediction.

We are going to do 5-fold cross-validation using the genomic data used for Tutorial 7 supplemented with some new data (400 random individuals) so that the Tutorial 7 data is Set 1 of 5.

There are several good graph plotting packages in R. I am going to use “lattice” at a later point in the tutorial, because it does what I want simply.

library("asreml")
## Online License checked out Tue Feb 14 21:52:03 2023
library("lattice")

QGM helper functions … nothing new …

source("qgm_helpers_t9.R")

2 Genotypes and relationship matrices

Now we have 500 individuals with genotypes and phenotypes. Each training set will have 400 individuals. You will remember that when we fitted models with a pedigree all the individuals in the pedigree had an EBV even though only a subset had phenotypes in the data in the model fitting, i.e. the training set was not the full pedigree. The same principle applies: if individuals are incorporated into the relationship matrix then we will get EBVs for them - the information from the training set on breeding values (and implicitly on SNP effects - Tutorial 8!) is spread across all the individuals for which we have genotypes using the relationships.

Although we will have to fit the model with different training sets for the cross-validation we only have to prepare \(\mathbf{G}\) once. Computationally we would be better off if we calculated the inverse of \(\mathbf{G}\) as well and provided that to asreml() rather than \(\mathbf{G}\) since it would save the repeated time-consuming inversion that will happen in the cross-validation (and this can be done), however for simplicity we are going to do it the long way. Inversion times increase with the cube of their size, so 5 times as big, is ~ 125 times longer, so in big problems this is not trivial.

We are not going to use both VR1 and VR2. We are going to use VR1 and think of SNP effects as allelic substitutions, therefore we will calculate \(W_1\) and \(G_1\). The structure of the genotypes is the same as Tutorial 7, except there are more individuals. The loci are labelled as before, and we will do a quick check to confirm there are no missing values.

2.1 Read and scan the genotypes

t9gen <- read.csv(file="rgen9.csv",header=TRUE,na.strings=c("NA"))
head(t9gen[,1:8])
##   id snp_1 snp_2 snp_3 snp_4 snp_5 snp_6 snp_7
## 1  1     1     2     2     1     2     2     0
## 2  2     2     0     1     1     2     0     0
## 3  3     0     1     1     1     1     0     1
## 4  4     1     2     0     2     2     1     0
## 5  5     0     1     1     2     1     1     0
## 6  6     1     1     1     1     1     0     1
tail(t9gen[,1:8])
##      id snp_1 snp_2 snp_3 snp_4 snp_5 snp_6 snp_7
## 495 495     0     1     0     2     2     1     1
## 496 496     1     0     1     1     2     1     2
## 497 497     1     1     0     1     1     1     1
## 498 498     0     1     1     2     0     0     0
## 499 499     0     0     1     2     0     1     1
## 500 500     0     1     0     2     2     0     1
loci <- c(2:501)
qgm_missing_snp(t9gen,loci)
## [1] "Number of loci with missing genotypes ... 0"
## [1] snp       n_missing p_missing
## <0 rows> (or 0-length row.names)
## [1] "Number of identities with missing genotypes ... 0"
## [1] id        n_missing p_missing
## <0 rows> (or 0-length row.names)
## [1] "Summary of missing genotypes completed"

For R code interest, one can check for duplicated SNPs or identical individuals. There aren’t any, but it has happened!

  • The first line looks for duplicated rows in the genotype data, which might occur with identical twins (say).
  • The second line looks for duplicated SNPs - the R code for this is not immediately obvious.
which(duplicated(t9gen[,loci]))
## integer(0)
which(duplicated(as.list(t9gen[,loci])))
## integer(0)

Everything looks OK. So we do the calculations of \(\mathbf{W}_1\) and create an invertible \(\mathbf{G}_1\) as before.

aaf <- qgm_counts_to_freq(t9gen[,loci])
cc <- sum(2.*aaf*(1.-aaf))
W1 <- qgm_vr1_to_W(t9gen[,loci],t9gen[,1],colnames(t9gen)[loci])
G1 <- qgm_W_to_G(W1,cc)
G1 <- G1+0.001*diag(nrow(G1))

Following Lecture 9, we shall check how closely the genetic variance among the individuals represented in \(\mathbf{G}_1\) is estimated by the \(V_g\) parameter estimated in asreml() by calculating \(\overline{diag(\mathbf{G}_1)}-\overline{\mathbf{G}_1}\).

mean(diag(G1))-mean(G1)
## [1] 0.9952358

As an illustration of genetic variation within a subset: the first 100 samples are those from Tutorial 7 - the scaling factor for this subset is obtained below.

mean(diag(G1[1:100,1:100]))-mean(G1[1:100,1:100])
## [1] 0.9982605
  • Both scaling factors are close to 1, so we will ignore corrections to \(V_g\) from here onwards.

2.2 Questions

  • Q1. Given the model that was simulated, why is it unsurprising that \(V_g\) estimated from \(\mathbf{G}_1\) will closely estimate the genetic variation among all the individuals represented in \(\mathbf{G}_1\).
  • \(\textbf{Answer.}\) The alleles at each locus for each individual were randomly sampled assuming no linkage. So the loci are expected to be in linkage equilibrium and the alleles ast each locus in HWE.

3 Reading the data

It is a simple structure again. Here set is not intended as a fixed effect but is the outcome of the random partition of the 500 data points into 5 equal sized sets.

  • In what follows we will be setting phenotypes to missing, set by set, so there is a column called spare which allows me to recreate the full set of the phenotypes.
  • The data includes the tbv, which is used for comparisons later.
  • I have an extra column called jid to avoid any computational complications from calling id a factor in R.
  • In cross-validation every data point is predicted in a validation set once, and only once. The column ebv is created to hold this value.
  • We will wish to calculate accuracies, so store the diagonal element for each individual in \(\mathbf{G}_1\) in t9dat.
  • In the course of the analysis below some extra working columns will be needed for receiving data from qgm_random_effects(); these have been called w.ebv, w.se, w.z, and w.jid
t9dat <- read.csv(file="rdat9.csv",header=TRUE,na.strings=c("NA"))
t9dat$gii <- diag(G1)
t9dat[,c("ebv","w.ebv","w.se","w.z","w.jid")] <- NA
t9dat$id <- factor(t9dat$id)
head(t9dat)
##   id set         phe        tbv jid       spare       gii ebv w.ebv w.se w.z w.jid
## 1  1   1  0.17902732 -0.8021403   1  0.17902732 1.0763765  NA    NA   NA  NA    NA
## 2  2   1 -1.12027769 -1.5363471   2 -1.12027769 0.9614231  NA    NA   NA  NA    NA
## 3  3   1  0.05917907  0.7822008   3  0.05917907 0.9623977  NA    NA   NA  NA    NA
## 4  4   1 -0.89490349 -0.1838609   4 -0.89490349 0.9934508  NA    NA   NA  NA    NA
## 5  5   1  1.83607902  0.4730611   5  1.83607902 0.9760422  NA    NA   NA  NA    NA
## 6  6   1 -1.03524851 -0.3384307   6 -1.03524851 1.0071961  NA    NA   NA  NA    NA

4 Set 1 as a validation set

4.1 Constructing the training set and fitting the model,

The code in the next major section sets up a for loop and a data frame to capture outcomes from doing the cross-validation. However before running it, let us go through the process using set 1 as the validation set (vset) and sets 2 to 5 as the training set (tset).

The key point is that set 1 data is set to missing so it does not influence the parameter estimates. We also calculate the estimated heritability which is 0.4673 (s.e. 0.0748) here.

vset <- t9dat$set==1 # helps code clarity, creating the list of rows in set 1
tset <- t9dat$set!=1 # helps code clarity, creating the list of rows in remaining sets
t9dat[vset,"phe"] <- NA # set the validation set as missing so they do not influence parameters
asr9 <- asreml(fixed= phe ~ 1, random= ~vm(id,G1), residual= ~idv(units), data= t9dat )
## Online License checked out Tue Feb 14 21:52:04 2023
## Model fitted using the sigma parameterization.
## ASReml 4.1.0 Tue Feb 14 21:52:04 2023
##           LogLik        Sigma2     DF     wall    cpu
##  1     -1487.158           1.0    399 21:52:05    0.1
##  2     -1099.994           1.0    399 21:52:05    0.1
##  3      -674.044           1.0    399 21:52:05    0.1
##  4      -379.034           1.0    399 21:52:05    0.1
##  5      -220.900           1.0    399 21:52:05    0.1
##  6      -175.716           1.0    399 21:52:05    0.1
##  7      -168.780           1.0    399 21:52:05    0.1
##  8      -168.495           1.0    399 21:52:05    0.1
##  9      -168.495           1.0    399 21:52:05    0.1
asr9 <- qgm_continue(asr9,10)
qgm_summary(asr9)
## [1] "CONVERGENCE"
## [1] "TRUE with logL = -168.494506079347"
## 
## [1] "VARIANCE COMPONENTS"
##             component  std.error  z.ratio bound %ch
## vm(id, G1)  0.4277467 0.08544490 5.006112     P   0
## units!units 0.4876044 0.06528636 7.468702     P   0
## units!R     1.0000000         NA       NA     F   0
## 
## [1] "FIXED EFFECTS"
##                 solution  std error   z.ratio
## (Intercept) -0.009576774 0.03541327 -0.270429
## 
## [1] "WALD TESTS"
## Model fitted using the sigma parameterization.
## Warning in asreml(fixed = phe ~ 1, random = ~vm(id, G1), residual = ~idv(units), : Algebraic derivatives for denominator df not
## available.
## ASReml 4.1.0 Tue Feb 14 21:52:07 2023
##           LogLik        Sigma2     DF     wall    cpu
##  1      -168.495           1.0    399 21:52:07    0.1
##  2      -168.495           1.0    399 21:52:07    0.1
## Calculating denominator DF
##             Df denDF   F.con        Pr
## (Intercept)  1 121.5 0.07313 0.7872891
hh <- vpredict(asr9, hh ~ V1/(V1+V2))
hh_hat <- hh$Estimate
hh
##     Estimate         SE
## hh 0.4673035 0.07478644
va <- vpredict(asr9, va ~ V1)
va_hat <- va$Estimate

4.2 Obtaining the predictions for the validation set

Extract the EBVs and copy these into the working variables in t9dat. The order of the rows should be identical. Having got the EBVs, the phe can be restored to the true values. Looking at t9dat$w.se one can observe that the prediction errors for the validation set are larger than the training set.

dum <- qgm_random_effects(asr9,"id")
colnames(dum) <- c("ebv","se","z","jid") 
t9dat[,c("w.ebv","w.se","w.z","w.jid")] <- dum

Restoring the phenotypes.

t9dat$phe <- t9dat$spare
head(t9dat)
##   id set         phe        tbv jid       spare       gii ebv       w.ebv      w.se        w.z w.jid
## 1  1   1  0.17902732 -0.8021403   1  0.17902732 1.0763765  NA -0.49315881 0.5645188 -0.8735914     1
## 2  2   1 -1.12027769 -1.5363471   2 -1.12027769 0.9614231  NA -0.16411208 0.5376448 -0.3052426     2
## 3  3   1  0.05917907  0.7822008   3  0.05917907 0.9623977  NA  0.17475870 0.5394773  0.3239408     3
## 4  4   1 -0.89490349 -0.1838609   4 -0.89490349 0.9934508  NA -0.07620408 0.5497944 -0.1386047     4
## 5  5   1  1.83607902  0.4730611   5  1.83607902 0.9760422  NA  0.25992292 0.5464000  0.4757008     5
## 6  6   1 -1.03524851 -0.3384307   6 -1.03524851 1.0071961  NA -0.35035599 0.5400772 -0.6487147     6
tail(t9dat)
##      id set         phe         tbv jid       spare       gii ebv       w.ebv      w.se        w.z w.jid
## 495 495   5 -0.31415559 -0.18386085 495 -0.31415559 0.9380493  NA -0.14972698 0.4239648 -0.3531590   495
## 496 496   5  0.43497165 -0.57028551 496  0.43497165 1.0524314  NA -0.05540192 0.4351148 -0.1273271   496
## 497 497   5 -0.99875935  0.39577613 497 -0.99875935 1.0611020  NA -0.28890819 0.4392748 -0.6576935   497
## 498 498   5  1.42132117  0.85948572 498  1.42132117 1.0882734  NA  0.64205423 0.4378865  1.4662570   498
## 499 499   5  0.65771989 -0.06793345 499  0.65771989 0.9571382  NA  0.51695412 0.4208443  1.2283738   499
## 500 500   5 -0.04725084  0.31849120 500 -0.04725084 1.0607155  NA -0.24751241 0.4388174 -0.5640442   500

4.3 Questions

  • Q2. Why do the s.e.s differ between individuals in the validation set and those in the training set?
  • \(\textbf{Answer.}\) One could give the simple \(\textbf{but true}\) answer that the training set individuals have information from their phenotype included in the parameter estimation. However I have realised the answer is far more subtle than I thought it was! Read on below, but realise that I might not have asked the question if I had understood just how subtle. I will explain the problem then the solution..
    • The benefit of genomic selection is to predict without the need of a phenotype! So what is happening?
    • Remember also that the Van Raden model has an equivalent SNP BLUP and the EBVs are simply \(\mathbf{W_1\hat{b}}\), where \(\mathbf{W_1}\) are the centred allele counts. The (co)variances of the EBVs are given by \(\mathbf{W_1V_bW_1}^T\) where \(\mathbf{V_b}\) is the (co)variance matrix of the allelic substitution effects of the loci. All individuals share the same loci and covariance matrix irrespective of whether they are in the training or validation set!
    • So why, then, is there a difference?
    • Now realise that there will be lots of covariances between the estimates as they try to explain the phenotypes of the training set, and the estimates and the covariances between them come as a result of finding the best fit of the SNPs to the phenotypes of the training sets. One can view the covariance of the SNP effects as how the estimation errors compensate each other to provide this best fit for the training set.
    • A new set of phenotypes in the validation set will not have the same array of allele frequencies and combinations of alleles across loci as the training set. The different allele frequencies mean that the more informative loci in the validation loci will not be as well estimated as the more informative loci in the training set. Just as importantly, the compensating estimation errors represented by the covariances are no longer compensating in exactly the right directions for the new phenotypes. This will mean the estimation errors for the validation set will be greater, i.e. the EBVs will be subject to greater uncertainty i.e. greater s.e.s.
    • Consequently it is quite subtle as to why the differences in the s.e. emerge!
    • So put in simple terms, the training set provides the phenotypes and consequently provides information on the estimates of allelic substitution \(\textbf{and}\) the (co)variance structure of the errors, consequently the EBVs for the training set have lower s.e.s.
    • This is so noticeable in this data set because it is small and so the estimates of marker effects still have substantial errors and covariances between these errors. As the data set becomes large these errors and covariances will diminish and this difference in s.e. will also diminish towards zero.
  • Q3. Write down the model equation for the model that produced asr9 above.
  • \(\textbf{Answer.}\)
    • Model is \(\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{1}m + \mathbf{Zu} + \mathbf{e}\) where
    • \(\mathbf{y}\) is (400x1) vector of phenotypes
    • \(\mathbf{1}\) is a (400x1) vector of 1’s (the design ‘matrix’ for the mean)
    • \(m\) is a scalar (i.e a single number) fitted mean
    • \(\mathbf{Z}\) is the (400x500) design matrix for the random effects \(\mathbf{u}\)
      • note that here \(\mathbf{Z}\) is \(\textbf{not}\) the Identity matrix, even though it is all 0’s and 1’s, as it has more columns that rows since the Set 1 individuals are included in the relationship matrix but have no phenotypes in \(\mathbf{y}\)
    • \(\mathbf{u}\) is a (400x1) vector of breeding values assumed to be random effects and distributed \(MVN(0, V_g \mathbf{G}_1)\), where \(\mathbf{G}_1\) is the Genomic Relationship matrix, calculated following Van Raden 1, and \(V_g\) is the additive genetic variance.
      • note I have used ‘additive genetic variance’ here, which is acceptable, as it IS an additive genetic variance. The fact it is a genic variance is not stressed in publications.
    • \(\mathbf{e}\) is a (400x1) vector of residuals assumed to be random effects and distributed \(MVN(0, V_e \mathbf{I})\), where \(\mathbf{I}\) is the (400x400) Identity matrix and \(V_e\) is the residual variance.

4.4 Model based accuracies

Consider the accuracies obtained assuming the model parameters are correct: the parameters assumed to be correct include the heritability and all the SNP effects. These are obtained as \(\sqrt{1- (se^2/(V_g \ g_{ii})}\) as we have done in previous tutorials. Since individuals within sets differ we will take the mean. This is done below for the validation set (vset) and training set (tset).

mean(sqrt(1.- t9dat[vset,]$w.se**2./(va_hat*t9dat[vset,]$gii)    ))
## [1] 0.5516212
mean(sqrt(1.- t9dat[tset,]$w.se**2./(va_hat*t9dat[tset,]$gii)    ))
## [1] 0.7528819

Plot the relationships between the EBVs and TBVs (a luxury with simulated data) and we can see that the difference in accuracy, the validation set have a wider scatter.

plot(t9dat[vset,]$w.ebv,t9dat[vset,]$tbv,col="red")
points(t9dat[tset,]$w.ebv,t9dat[tset,]$tbv,col="grey")

4.5 What NOT to do and WHY!

As a demonstration of what NOT to do, and WHY, we will look at ‘accuracy’ by correlating the training set with their phenotypes and then scaling by the square root of \(\hat{h^2}\) (see Lecture 8). The ‘accuracy’ is a correlation (and so lies between -1 and 1) is \(corr(\hat{g},g)\) and after scaling is 1.32! The ‘bias’ is the slope of the TBV on the EBV, and in real data this is estimated from phenotypes in one form or another, is 1.70 (s.e. 0.04). This slope should be 1 for no bias!

These estimates of accuracy from the correlation in the training set and the bias using the training set are meaningless! This is because the phenotypes have been used to estimate the parameters in the model and these ‘best fit’ parameters are explaining as much of the variation in the phenotypes as possible given the model, and so the parameter estimates become (favourably) correlated with the environmental deviations within the phenotypes - remember the estimates are linear functions of the phenotypes in the training set derived by our complex algorithms.

This demonstrates the golden rule of validations: the validation set should NEVER influence the parameter estimates in any way.

cor(t9dat[tset,]$w.ebv,t9dat[tset,]$phe)/sqrt(hh_hat)
## [1] 1.319589
## attr(,"gradient")
##             V1         V2 V3
## [1,] 0.5819587 -0.5105183  0
summary(lm(phe ~ 1 + w.ebv, data = t9dat[tset,]))
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = phe ~ 1 + w.ebv, data = t9dat[tset, ])
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -1.35130 -0.26376 -0.01358  0.23175  1.43082 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) -0.01056    0.02091  -0.505    0.614    
## w.ebv        1.69808    0.04072  41.697   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.4181 on 398 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.8137, Adjusted R-squared:  0.8133 
## F-statistic:  1739 on 1 and 398 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

Finally in this detailed examination, look at the estimates of accuracy and bias from the validation set - these DO have meaning! The accuracy estimated for this set is 0.469, lower than that assuming the model is correct. The regression coefficient for detecting bias is 0.816 (s.e. 0.243).

cor(t9dat[vset,]$w.ebv,t9dat[vset,]$phe)/sqrt(hh_hat)
## [1] 0.4701647
## attr(,"gradient")
##             V1         V2 V3
## [1,] 0.5819587 -0.5105183  0
summary(lm(phe ~ 1 + w.ebv, data = t9dat[vset,]))
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = phe ~ 1 + w.ebv, data = t9dat[vset, ])
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -2.08628 -0.61378 -0.03211  0.60473  2.50894 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)   
## (Intercept)  0.02591    0.09256    0.28  0.78010   
## w.ebv        0.81570    0.24277    3.36  0.00111 **
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.9255 on 98 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.1033, Adjusted R-squared:  0.09415 
## F-statistic: 11.29 on 1 and 98 DF,  p-value: 0.001112

We can carry out a significance test for bias using the slope (\(\hat{b}\)). This is H0: \(\hat{b}\) = 1 against H1: \(\hat{b} \ne 1\). This is a 2-sided Student’s t-test.

  • Calculate the deviation from H0 i.e. 1: \(\hat{b}-1\) = 0.816-1 = -0.184.
  • Divide by the s.e. of \(\hat{b}\) i.e. -0.184/0.243 = -0.757
  • Compare to a t-distribution with df as shown in the lm() summary (2 less than the number of data points).
  • For this 2-sided test, compare the absolute value (i.e. 0.757) to the 0.975 point for P<0.05, to the 0.995 point for P<0.01, and the 0.9995 point for P<0.001 - corresponding to Type 1 errors of 0.05, 0.01 and 0.001.
-0.184/0.243
## [1] -0.7572016
qt(0.975,98,lower.tail=TRUE)
## [1] 1.984467
qt(0.995,98,lower.tail=TRUE)
## [1] 2.626931
qt(0.9995,98,lower.tail=TRUE)
## [1] 3.392588

The conclusion is that there is no evidence of bias in this set.

5 Five-fold cross-validation

5.1 Setting up a data frame to collect results together

We could simply repeat the code above for every set and collect the results together - that is perfectly OK. The code below is more complex, but it is done for the purpose of simplifying and presenting the work for the tutorial. It is too specific for this tutorial to be a qgm helper function.

I am going to summarise some results in a separate data frame with one row for each set. From here onwards the training set outcomes will be ignored as only the results from the validation set have any validity. I include some estimates of accuracy and bias using the real TBVs for comparisons, a luxury that is not possible in real data! What is being collected is:

  • set … for obvious reasons;
  • r_m … the accuracy obtained from the s.e.s in the asr output, which assume the model is correct;
  • r_p … the accuracy calculated using the phenotypes of the validation set;
  • r_t … the accuracy in the validation set using the true TBV;
  • b_p … the bias calculated using the phenotypes of the validation set;
  • b_t … the bias in the validation set calculated using the TBV;
  • hh_hat … the estimate of heritability from the training set used (following Lecture 9);
  • hh … the true heritability in the validation set calculated from the variances of the TBVs and the phenotypes in the set.
t9vs <- data.frame("set"=integer(),"r_m"=numeric(),"r_p"=numeric(),"r_t"=numeric(),"b_p"=numeric(),"b_t"=numeric(),"hh_hat"=numeric(),"hh"=numeric())

5.2 Carrying out the full cross-validation

In thefor loop below the validation is carried out for each set in turn. The code is NOT important - it is not necessarily good, but it works and it simplifies the tutorial!

  • The EBVs for the validation set are transferred from w.ebv to ebv so at the end ebv carries the EBV for each individual that was estimated when it was part of the validation set.
  • summary(asr9)$varcomp[1]$component[1] extracts the estimate from the asr9. Normally this has been done by qgm_summary()
  • summary(lm(phe ~ 1 + ebv, data=t9dat[vset,]))$coef[2] extracts the slope of the regression.
nset <- 5
for(i in 1:nset){
  vset <- t9dat$set==i
  tset <- t9dat$set!=i
  # set the phenotypes to missing for analysis
  t9dat[vset,]$phe <- NA
  # run analysis ... note that G1 does not have to change as it always has all individuals
  asr9 <- asreml(fixed= phe ~ 1, random= ~vm(id,G1), residual= ~idv(units), data= t9dat,       trace=FALSE)
  # a check just to make sure of convergence
  asr9 <- qgm_continue(asr9,10)
  # calculate estimate of heritability for the set
  hh_hat <- vpredict(asr9, hh ~ V1/(V1+V2))$Estimate
  # extract data and join to t9dat for calculations
  dum <- qgm_random_effects(asr9,"id")
  colnames(dum) <- c("ebv","se","z","jid") 
  t9dat[,c("w.ebv","w.se","w.z","w.jid")] <- dum
  # check no error is made
  if(!identical(t9dat[,"jid"],t9dat[,"w.jid"])) {
    print("listing error in set ",i) 
  }
  # store the ebv obtained for the validation set for future use
  t9dat[vset,]$ebv <- t9dat[vset,]$w.ebv 
  # restore the phenotypes
  t9dat$phe <- t9dat$spare
  # gather results for the validation set
  dum <- t9dat[vset,]
  t9vs[i,"set"] <- i 
  t9vs[i,"r_m"] <- mean(sqrt(1.-dum$w.se*dum$w.se/hh_hat)) # accuracy when model is correct
  t9vs[i,"r_p"] <- cor(dum$phe,dum$ebv)/sqrt(hh_hat) # accuracy with phenotypes
  t9vs[i,"r_t"] <- cor(dum$tbv,dum$ebv) # true accuracy for validation set
  t9vs[i,"b_p"] <- summary(lm(phe ~ 1 + ebv, data=dum))$coef[2] # the bias with phenotypes
  t9vs[i,"b_t"] <- summary(lm(tbv ~ 1 + ebv, data=dum))$coef[2] # the true bias
  t9vs[i,"hh_hat"] <- hh_hat
  t9vs[i,"hh"] <- var(dum$tbv)/var(dum$phe) # true heritability in set
  print(paste0("Finished set ... ",i))
}
## [1] "Finished set ... 1"
## [1] "Finished set ... 2"
## [1] "Finished set ... 3"
## [1] "Finished set ... 4"
## [1] "Finished set ... 5"

5.3 Looking at the outcome

The data frame below shows a summary of what was obtained.

t9vs
##   set       r_m       r_p       r_t       b_p       b_t    hh_hat        hh
## 1   1 0.6022475 0.4701647 0.5385987 0.8157021 0.9939444 0.4673035 0.5287245
## 2   2 0.5627819 0.4790526 0.6112565 0.7180534 1.0326122 0.4714777 0.5988827
## 3   3 0.5925480 0.6846451 0.5632185 1.3946976 1.1651150 0.4143124 0.4272505
## 4   4 0.6131066 0.5729846 0.5900703 0.9693277 1.0585955 0.4903241 0.5514176
## 5   5 0.5931105 0.4843238 0.5451633 0.9231182 1.0233456 0.4304626 0.4175263
round(colMeans(t9vs[,2:8]),3)
##    r_m    r_p    r_t    b_p    b_t hh_hat     hh 
##  0.593  0.538  0.570  0.964  1.055  0.455  0.505

5.4 Discussion of t9vs

We can draw some broad conclusions.

  • We might generally expect that the accuracy obtained assuming the model and estimates are correct (r_m) will be higher than the one obtained using the validation phenotypes, as they are here. Here the model used for the analysis is the true model used for the simulation, and both r_m and r_p are both close to the actual accuracies r_t. Deviations are likely due to chance estimation errors. Both r_m and r_p have estimation errors:
    • r_m assumes all the model parameters are correct including \(h^2\) which is used for scaling, but they do have estimation errors (look at Tutorial 8!);
    • r_p will depend (i) on the phenotypes sampled with their environmental errors, and (ii) will depend on the sample size as small samples will have larger errors attached to the estimated slopes and correlations. The sampling error of a simple correlation with \(n\) samples is \(\approx 1/\sqrt{n}\) - while this is not exact it is useful for planning!
  • A major assumption (beyond those of our usual \(P = G + E\) assumption) in genomic evaluation models is that the markers capture all the genetic variance. Typically we rely on LD of anonymous markers with the causal variants unknown, and we assume that we capture all the genetic variance. If we do not then r_m must be optimistic as it underestimates the total genetic variation and the phenotype is based on the full breeding value. Here \(\textit{in our simulated data}\) our markers do capture all the variance.
  • One conclusion is that both r_m and r_p are of value, and (assuming the validation set is large enough) differences are informative.
  • The conclusion from the bias is that the EBVs show no evidence of biased, neither b_m nor b_p.
  • b_p and r_p tend to be more variable compared to b_t and r_t, and I interpret this as being due to the variation arising from sampling the phenotypes in what is a relatively small sample size.

5.5 Summarising the EBVs collected over all the sets

We summarise ebv in t9dat in three ways:

  • Giving a scatter plot for all the points, coloured by set, with ebv on the x-axis, and either tbv or phe on the y-axis. Plotted on the same scales to make sure they are easy to compare. This is the command from lattice … there are other ways.
  • The accuracies judged by scaled phenotypes or TBV.
  • The bias judged by scaled phenotypes or TBV.
xyplot(phe ~ ebv, groups=set, data=t9dat, xlim=c(-1.5,1.5),ylim=c(-3.25,3.25))

xyplot(tbv ~ ebv ,groups=set, data=t9dat, xlim=c(-1.5,1.5),ylim=c(-3.25,3.25))

cor(t9dat$ebv,t9dat$phe)/sqrt(0.4561)
cor(t9dat$ebv,t9dat$tbv)
summary(lm(phe ~ 1 + ebv, data=t9dat))$coef[2]
summary(lm(tbv ~ 1 + ebv, data=t9dat))$coef[2]
## [1] 0.5362465
## [1] 0.57123
## [1] 0.94758
## [1] 1.055993

Discussion of the outcomes.

  • Remember we are fitting the right model for the data!
  • The plots show:
    • the moderate predictive accuracy obtained explaining about 0.3 of the variance in TBV (i.e. 0.3 ~ accuracy squared).
    • demonstrate the extra scatter going from TBV to phenotype arising from the environmental noise;
    • good randomisation.
  • The true accuracy is ~0.57 over all the data, and the complete cross-validation data was 0.54, so it is giving good guidance.
  • The EBVs are unbiased as assessed by the TBVs, and the cross-validation is 0.92 but not very different.

5.6 Questions

  • Q4. Carry out a test to see if the regression of t9dat$phe on t9dat$ebv gives evidence of bias in the EBVs in the full validation set with Type 1 error =0.05.
    • Note this is another way of summarising the cross-validation: pooling the predicted values for each data point when it was in the validation set and look at the accuracy and bias.
  • \(\textbf{Answer.}\) If \(\beta\) is the slope, we test H0: \(\beta = 1\) against and alternative hypothesis H1: \(\beta \ne 1\), a two-sided test. The test statistic is (0.948-1)/0.109 = -0.052/0.109 = -0.477. This is compared to Student’s t with 498 d.f. which has a critical value of 1.965 for a Type 1 error of 0.05. Therefore there is no evidence this there is bias in the EBVs (P>0.05).
summary(lm(phe ~ 1 + ebv, data=t9dat))
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = phe ~ 1 + ebv, data = t9dat)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -2.71000 -0.54007 -0.01431  0.61319  2.82241 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) -0.01275    0.04040  -0.316    0.752    
## ebv          0.94758    0.10929   8.670   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.9029 on 498 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.1312, Adjusted R-squared:  0.1294 
## F-statistic: 75.18 on 1 and 498 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
0.052/0.109
## [1] 0.4770642
qt(0.975,498)
## [1] 1.964739

6 Receiver Operator Characteristic (ROC) Curves

6.1 Setting up disease phenotypes

ROC curves are useful when assessing a continuous predictor for use in predicting a dichotomous outcome, i.e. two categories. We will look at ROC curves by assuming that phe is the liability of disease risk and so that all individual above a threshold for phe become ‘cases’ (TRUE) and the remainder are ‘controls’ (FALSE) - that is how a liability model works, those with the highest phenotypic liability are those that succumb to the disease. In the our simulated disease all the phenotypes >1.1 will be the diseased individuals.

6.2 Questions

  • Q5. What is the incidence of the disease in our sample?
  • \(\textbf{Answer.}\) It is 63, which represents a fraction 0.126.
hist(t9dat$phe)

t9dat$dis <- t9dat$phe>1.1
head(t9dat$dis)
## [1] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE  TRUE FALSE
sum(t9dat$dis)
## [1] 63
sum(is.na(t9dat$dis)) # no missing values
## [1] 0
sum(t9dat$dis)/nrow(t9dat) 
## [1] 0.126

6.3 Some helper functions for ROC curves

To explore ROC curves we introduce two more qgm helper functions qgm_roc() and qgm_auc() (you can get R packages on ROC curves, but the functions are not very complicated).

  • qgm_roc() takes in a data frame with two columns, the first is the predictor (in our case our predicted genetic values, the validation EBVs), and the second is our column of case/control data. qgm_roc() returns a data frame with 3 columns in the same order as the input with the predictor, sensitivity se, and specificity sp associated with the value of the predictor.
  • qgm_auc() takes in the 3 columns output by qgm_roc() and returns the area under the curve (AUC).
qgm_roc <- function(df) {
  df$out_rank <- c(1:nrow(df))
  df <- df[order(df[,1]),]
  nill <- sum(df[,2])
  nfit <- nrow(df)-nill
  df$se <- 1.-cumsum(df[,2])/nill
  df$sp <- cumsum(!df[,2])/nfit
  return(df[order(df$out_rank),c(1,4,5)])
}
qgm_auc <- function(df) {
  df <- df[order(df[,1]),]
  df$dx[1] <- df[,3][1]
  df$dx[2:nrow(df)] <- df[,3][2:nrow(df)]-df[,3][1:(nrow(df)-1)]
  return(sum(df$dx*df[,2]))
}

6.4 The ROC curves

The ROC curve is the plot of Sensitivity on the y-axis against 1-Specificity on the x-axis. Our validation EBVs from our data give an area under the curve of 0.699.

epi <- qgm_roc(t9dat[,c("ebv","dis")])
head(epi)
##           ebv        se         sp
## 1 -0.49315881 0.9841270 0.09153318
## 2 -0.16411208 0.8730159 0.35240275
## 3  0.17475870 0.6031746 0.70251716
## 4 -0.07620408 0.8412698 0.42791762
## 5  0.25992292 0.4444444 0.79405034
## 6 -0.35035599 0.9682540 0.17391304
plot(1.-epi[,"sp"],epi[,"se"])
lines(x=c(0,1),y=c(0,1))

auc <- qgm_auc(epi)
auc
## [1] 0.6992844

6.5 Questions

  • Q6. In UK we are concerned about para-tuberculosis in cows (different from bovine TB). We may guess there is genetic variation in susceptibility. Suppose the genetic predictor was used as a population-wide screening test for liability, followed with selection by banning all cows that fail the test from breeding in an effort to control the epidemic. We wish to manage the additional costs of compensation to breeders so that at most 10% of healthy cows would be banned in error, what fraction of disease cases might you you catch?
  • \(\textbf{Answer.}\) If we wish to use the test and restrict the false positives to 10% of the healthy population i.e. have a specificity of 0.9, the we need to examine the ROC curve with 1-Sp = 0.1. We could read the ROC curve to see the estimated sensitivity (the true positives as a fraction of the diseased) would be ~ 0.3, i.e. only 30% of disease cases would be caught. This is better than random, but not impressive! To calculate the estimated value more exactly, we can use the code below. It gives the estimate as 27% where sp first falls below 0.9 i.e. 1-sp first exceed 0.1. Remember specificity is the true negatives as a fraction of the healthy, so false positives as a fraction of the healthy is one minus the specificity.
epi <- epi[order(epi[,"se"],-epi[,"sp"]),]
head(epi[epi[,"sp"]<0.90,])
##           ebv        se        sp
## 350 0.4112705 0.2698413 0.8993135
## 240 0.4052964 0.2698413 0.8970252
## 443 0.4050091 0.2698413 0.8947368
## 253 0.4020944 0.2698413 0.8924485
## 458 0.3975781 0.2857143 0.8924485
## 396 0.3937004 0.3015873 0.8924485
sum(epi$ebv>0.4112705) # number culled 
## [1] 61
  • Q7. Using the luxury of simulated data, give an estimate of the limit to the quality of the predictor for selection (as judged by AUC) if we were to have a much bigger training set. Hint: if we had a much bigger what would the ebvs look like?
  • \(\textbf{Answer.}\) We can do this with the TBV which we have in simulated data! When training sets are large we would hope the EBVs get very close to the TBV - of course if the markers are not capturing the whole of the genetic variance this answer will be optimistic! The AUC (area under the curve) becomes 0.868, which begins to look quite good.
epi <- qgm_roc(t9dat[,c("tbv","dis")])
head(epi)
##          tbv        se          sp
## 1 -0.8021403 1.0000000 0.141876430
## 2 -1.5363471 1.0000000 0.004576659
## 3  0.7822008 0.5238095 0.922196796
## 4 -0.1838609 0.9523810 0.471395881
## 5  0.4730611 0.7301587 0.832951945
## 6 -0.3384307 0.9523810 0.345537757
plot(1.-epi[,"sp"],epi[,"se"])

auc <- qgm_auc(epi)
auc
## [1] 0.8675311
  • Q8. How much does your answer to Q6 change with a bigger training set?
  • \(\textbf{Answer.}\) We would capture twice as many, 54% of diseased cows instead of 27%. This can be seen from the ROC curve or below.
epi <- epi[order(epi[,"se"],-epi[,"sp"]),]
head(epi[epi[,"sp"]<0.90,])
##           tbv        se        sp
## 31  0.7049159 0.5396825 0.8993135
## 458 0.6662734 0.5555556 0.8993135
## 283 0.6662734 0.5714286 0.8993135
## 466 0.6662734 0.5873016 0.8993135
## 221 0.6662734 0.6031746 0.8993135
## 481 0.6276309 0.6031746 0.8970252
sum(epi$tbv>0.7049159) # number culled 
## [1] 73
  • Q9. Explore the relationship of the AUC with the disease prevalence (i.e. how frequent is it in the population) by changing the threshold for disease above.
  • \(\textbf{Answer.}\) For the purpose of presentation in the tutorial this is done in a for loop and collected together in a data frame. There is a hint that the AUC decreases with incidence.
t9roc <- data.frame("threshold"=numeric(),"incidence"=numeric(),"AUC_with_EBV"=numeric(),"AUC_with_TBV"=numeric())
for(i in 1:13) {
  t9roc[i,"threshold"] <- (i-1)*0.1
  t9dat$dis <- t9dat$phe>t9roc[i,"threshold"]
  t9roc[i,"incidence"] <- sum(t9dat$dis)/nrow(t9dat)
  epi <- qgm_roc(t9dat[,c("ebv","dis")])
  t9roc[i,"AUC_with_EBV"] <- qgm_auc(epi)
  epi <- qgm_roc(t9dat[,c("tbv","dis")])
  t9roc[i,"AUC_with_TBV"] <- qgm_auc(epi)
}
t9roc
##    threshold incidence AUC_with_EBV AUC_with_TBV
## 1        0.0     0.528    0.6726855    0.8190646
## 2        0.1     0.486    0.6622312    0.8166402
## 3        0.2     0.426    0.6503574    0.8217435
## 4        0.3     0.386    0.6479384    0.8253025
## 5        0.4     0.344    0.6532012    0.8285238
## 6        0.5     0.290    0.6723069    0.8363866
## 7        0.6     0.252    0.6949750    0.8510313
## 8        0.7     0.228    0.7037769    0.8631488
## 9        0.8     0.194    0.7275588    0.8742677
## 10       0.9     0.164    0.7014821    0.8739351
## 11       1.0     0.146    0.6910911    0.8763594
## 12       1.1     0.126    0.6992844    0.8675311
## 13       1.2     0.106    0.7073150    0.8581740

7 Outcomes

  • We have seen how to carry out a validation using a validation set to assess accuracy and bias.
  • We have seen how to conduct a cross-validation.
  • We have explored different measures of accuracy.
  • We have explored the use of ROC curves when our predictor is predicting a 0/1 outcome.
